skill.name: high-quality-commit
skill.description: Guides AI coding assistants through git commit strategy selection, commit message quality, and post-commit verification for repository changes.
skill.summary: Create clear git commits with strategy guidance for squash, new commits, and history cleanup.
skill.author: AKABANAKK

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command patterns because the skill documents git workflows in markdown. AI review found no malicious intent, no prompt injection, and no exfiltration behavior, but confirmed operational risk from history rewriting, broad staging, force push, and hard reset guidance.

medium_findings.title: Git History Rewriting Requires User Approval
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends amend, interactive rebase, and force-with-lease workflows. These are legitimate git operations, but they can rewrite published history and disrupt collaborators if used without explicit approval.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit git history operations in documented workflows. They are not malicious, but the operational risk is clear when used on shared branches.

medium_findings.title: Broad Staging Can Include Unrelated Files
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly recommends staging all changes. This may commit unrelated local changes or secrets when the working tree contains files outside the intended task.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is documented many times and can affect repository contents. The risk depends on local working tree state, so confidence is high but not absolute.

medium_findings.title: Hard Reset Guidance Can Discard Work
medium_findings.description: The troubleshooting guide recommends a hard reset to recover from an accidental amend. This can delete uncommitted work and should require confirmation before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hard reset command is directly present in the troubleshooting section. It is a legitimate recovery command, but its data-loss risk is well understood.

low_findings.title: Static Crypto And Reconnaissance Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance alerts point to markdown examples about authentication concepts and git status inspection. No implementation, network request, credential access, or prompt injection text was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are prose or markdown command examples, not executable skill code. I found no semantic evidence of cryptographic implementation or system reconnaissance intent.

dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends amend, interactive rebase, and force-with-lease workflows. These are legitimate git operations, but they can rewrite published history and disrupt collaborators if used without explicit approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit git history operations in documented workflows. They are not malicious, but the operational risk is clear when used on shared branches.

dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is documented many times and can affect repository contents. The risk depends on local working tree state, so confidence is high but not absolute.

dangerous_patterns.description: The troubleshooting guide recommends a hard reset to recover from an accidental amend. This can delete uncommitted work and should require confirmation before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hard reset command is directly present in the troubleshooting section. It is a legitimate recovery command, but its data-loss risk is well understood.

content.value_statement: Messy commit history slows review and makes rollback harder. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code choose a commit strategy and write clear messages.
actual_capabilities: Reviews branch status and commit history before choosing a strategy.
Recommends squash, new commit, or interactive rebase based on change scope.
Guides conventional commit messages with subject, body, and footer sections.
Provides examples for review fixes, WIP cleanup, and staged feature commits.
Checks the latest commit and repository status after the commit is created.
limitations: Requires a local git repository and permission to run git commands.
History rewriting can disrupt collaborators after commits are pushed.
Does not verify tests, CI results, or code correctness by itself.
Should not run on protected branches without explicit user approval.

use_cases.title: Polish a Feature Branch
use_cases.description: Choose whether to amend, create a new commit, or reorganize work before opening a pull request.

use_cases.title: Prepare Review History
use_cases.description: Convert WIP commits into logical units that reviewers can understand and test independently.

use_cases.title: Finalize AI Coding Changes
use_cases.description: Ask Claude, Codex, or Claude Code to package completed changes into a clear repository commit.

prompt_templates.title: Commit Current Work
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my current git changes and create an appropriate commit. Explain the strategy before running any history rewriting command.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Commit Message
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the staged changes and draft a concise commit message with a subject, body, and issue reference when useful.

prompt_templates.title: Clean Review Feedback
prompt_templates.prompt: I addressed pull request feedback. Decide whether these changes should amend the existing commit or become a separate commit.

prompt_templates.title: Rework Branch History
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this feature branch against origin/main and propose a safe rebase plan. Ask before rewriting published history.

output_examples.input: A branch contains one feature commit plus small review fixes.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends amending the feature commit, updates the message to mention validation changes, and verifies status afterward.

output_examples.input: A branch contains several WIP commits for one feature.
output_examples.output: The assistant proposes grouping related commits into two logical changes and asks before starting interactive rebase.

output_examples.input: A working tree includes a bug fix and new tests.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends one focused fix commit and explains why the tests belong with the bug fix.

best_practices: Review the working tree before staging files.
Ask before amending, rebasing, force pushing, or resetting history.
Match the repository commit style and keep each commit logically focused.
anti_patterns: Running broad staging without checking for unrelated files or secrets.
Rewriting commits that other collaborators may already use.
Mixing unrelated features, fixes, and documentation changes in one commit.

faq.question: Does this skill run git commands?
faq.answer: Yes. It guides git status, commit, amend, rebase, push, and reset workflows. Users should approve risky commands first.

faq.question: Is it safe for shared branches?
faq.answer: Use caution. Amend, rebase, force push, and hard reset can affect collaborators when history is already shared.

faq.question: Can it write commit messages?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides conventional commit guidance and examples for subjects, bodies, footers, and issue references.

faq.question: Can it clean up WIP commits?
faq.answer: Yes. It can propose an interactive rebase plan to squash, reorder, or drop local work in logical groups.

faq.question: Does it check for secrets before committing?
faq.answer: No. Users should run their normal secret scanning and review files before broad staging commands.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: The marketplace metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
